did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used the quantusSKIN system, a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) based on an inception algorithm variant and a fully convolutional network (FCN-8s) for segmentation, to analyze dermoscopic images and diagnose melanoma versus benign lesions in a hospital population.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The study is an original experimental investigation. It describes a retrospective analysis of 232 dermoscopic images from a hospital population to evaluate the efficacy (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) of the quantusSKIN deep learning system for melanoma diagnosis. It includes specific methods (image segmentation, CNN retraining), statistical analysis, and original results (AUC 0.813, sensitivity 0.691, specificity 0.802), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Efficacy of a Deep Learning Convolutional Neural Network System for Melanoma Diagnosis in a Hospital Population
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
brief_summary: This study evaluated the efficacy of the quantusSKIN deep learning system, a convolutional neural network based on an inception algorithm variant and FCN-8s segmentation, for distinguishing melanoma from benign nevi in a hospital population. The retrospective analysis utilized 232 dermoscopic images from the Ramón y Cajal University Hospital database, with histopathology as the gold standard. The system was retrained using 339 additional images from the same hospital to improve performance beyond its initial training on the ISIC Archive. Evaluation via ROC analysis yielded an area under the curve of 0.813, with an optimum diagnostic threshold of 67.33% providing a sensitivity of 0.691, specificity of 0.802, and accuracy of 0.776. The results suggest that retraining CNN models with specific hospital data improves detection efficacy. Consequently, the quantusSKIN system is proposed as a useful screening tool for melanoma detection in primary health care systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included both in situ and invasive types, except amelanotic, subungual, mucosal, or ocular melanomas. Diagnosed via histopathologic examination as gold standard. n=55 in testing set, n=143 in retraining set.
Benign skin lesions. Diagnosed by dermoscopy with follow-up or histopathology. n=177 in testing set, n=196 in retraining set.
General category used for binary diagnosis (benign/malignant) alongside melanoma. The study distinguishes between benign skin lesions and melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of melanoma versus benign skin lesions (nevus)
all_task_types.details: The quantusSKIN system performs binary classification to distinguish between benign skin lesions and melanoma. The model outputs a probabilistic percentage (diagnostic threshold) for melanoma diagnosis, which is used to determine the class label based on an optimum diagnostic threshold (e.g., 67.33%). The study evaluates classification performance metrics including sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, PPV, NPV, and F1 score.

datasets_used.name: Hospital Universitario Ramón y Cajal Clinical Database
datasets_used.description: A private hospital dataset containing dermoscopic images from the clinical practice of the Service of Dermatology at Hospital Universitario Ramón y Cajal in Madrid, Spain. Images were captured using DermLite II PROHR 3GEN, DL200 HR, DL4W, and FotoFinder devices.

use.training: 339 images were used to retrain the quantusSKIN system's convolutional neural network to improve detection efficacy for this specific hospital population.
use.testing: 232 images were used to evaluate the efficacy (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC) of the retrained quantusSKIN system against histopathology as the gold standard.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (2019 and 2020)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration used for the initial training of the quantusSKIN system's deep learning model.

use.training: 37,688 biopsy-verified images were used for the original training of the quantusSKIN system before the current study's retraining phase.
use.other: Served as the base model source for the quantusSKIN system prior to retraining with hospital-specific data.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the normality of the distributions of the variables.
To compare the diagnostic threshold offered by the quantusSKIN system and the age between the nevus and melanoma groups.
To analyze the association between gender and type of skin lesion in the total sample.

To quantify the sensitivity and specificity of the quantusSKIN system to diagnose melanoma in comparison with histological diagnosis as the gold standard, and to determine the optimum diagnostic threshold.

hypothesis_testing.test: Student’s t-test for independent samples; Chi-square test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the mean diagnostic threshold or age between the nevus and melanoma groups (for t-test); There is no association between gender and type of skin lesion (for chi-square test).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in the mean diagnostic threshold or age between the nevus and melanoma groups (for t-test); There is an association between gender and type of skin lesion (for chi-square test).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study established a statistical significance of 95% (p < 0.05). It found statistically significant differences in age (p < 0.001) and diagnostic threshold (p < 0.001) between the nevus and melanoma groups, and no significant association between gender and lesion type (p = 0.060).

Hospital Universitario Ramón y Cajal Cli.description: Retrospective analysis of 232 dermoscopic images (177 nevi, 55 melanomas) from 222 patients to evaluate the quantusSKIN system.
Hospital Universitario Ramón y Cajal Cli.notes: The mean diagnostic threshold was statistically lower (p < 0.001) in the nevus group compared with the melanoma group. Retraining improved sensitivity (from 0.655 to 0.691) and accuracy (from 0.767 to 0.776) while maintaining specificity at 0.802 for the threshold >0.800 setting. False negatives were 30.9% and false positives were 19.8% at the 67.33% threshold after retraining.

Comparison with External Studies (Litera.description: Comparison of quantusSKIN efficacy against previously reported deep learning algorithms.
Comparison with External Studies (Litera.notes: Haenssle et al. reported better results than the current study. Brinker et al. reported sensitivity of 0.682. Kaur et al. reported accuracy of 0.830 and specificity of 0.839.

architecture_used.pretraining: Originally trained with 37,688 biopsy-verified images from the ISIC Archive 2019 and 2020. Retrained in this study with 339 additional images from the Hospital Universitario Ramón y Cajal clinical database.

architecture_details.classification_model: Convolutional neural network with a variant of the inception algorithm

training_details.early_stopping: Loss not improved for 10 consecutive epochs

preprocessing_techniques: Image selection criteria: images were required to be well-centered with isolated skin lesions occupying at least two-thirds of image dimensions, and free of light filters, measurement marks, or hair covering the lesion.
Automatic segmentation: a fully convolutional network (FCN-8s) was used to automatically delimitate the skin lesion in each image.
Data augmentation during training: random flipping, random cropping (0–10%), random translation (0–10 pixels), and random rotation (-90 to 90 degrees).

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to training images at each batch.
Applied to training images at each batch with a range of 0–10%.
Applied to training images at each batch with a range of 0–10 pixels.
Applied to training images at each batch with a range of -90 to 90 degrees.

conclusions: The quantusSKIN deep learning system demonstrated an area under the ROC curve of 0.813 for distinguishing melanoma from benign nevi in a hospital population.
At an optimum diagnostic threshold of 67.33%, the quantusSKIN system achieved a sensitivity of 0.691, specificity of 0.802, and accuracy of 0.776.

Retraining the quantusSKIN system with specific hospital data (339 images) improved its detection efficacy, increasing sensitivity from 0.655 to 0.691 and accuracy from 0.767 to 0.776 compared to the model trained only on public ISIC Archive data.

The study concludes that convolutional neural network models should be retrained with images from the specific medical consultations where they are used, rather than relying solely on public databases, to account for factors such as different skin phenotypes.
The quantusSKIN system is proposed as a useful screening tool for melanoma detection to be incorporated into primary health care systems.
A limitation of the study is that the influence of skin color on the efficacy of the quantusSKIN system was not investigated.

Future studies should address limitations regarding image quality variations (lighting, filters, magnification), different skin lesion characteristics (hair presence), and the need to retrain the system with images from conventional devices like smartphones to check reproducibility.
important_remarks: The study population consisted exclusively of white skin patients (n=513), and the influence of skin color on efficacy was not investigated, representing a significant limitation regarding generalizability to diverse skin phenotypes.
The dataset was small (232 testing images from 222 patients) and derived from a single hospital in Madrid, Spain, limiting external validation and introducing potential population bias.

The model was retrained using data from the same institution where it was tested (Hospital Universitario Ramón y Cajal), raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting to specific local imaging protocols and patient demographics.

The system demonstrated a high false negative rate of 30.9% for melanoma at the chosen diagnostic threshold (67.33%), which is a critical safety concern for a screening tool intended to minimize referrals.
The study compared the system's performance against literature baselines (Haenssle, Brinker, Kaur) rather than against dermatologists, making direct clinical efficacy comparisons difficult.

The training and testing images were strictly selected to be well-centered, isolated, and free of hair or light filters, which may not reflect the variability and quality issues encountered in real-world primary care settings.
The study excluded specific melanoma subtypes (amelanotic, subungual, mucosal, ocular), limiting the scope of the system's validated diagnostic capabilities.

The system is designed for primary health care screening rather than specialized diagnosis, prioritizing specificity and negative predictive value (NPV) over sensitivity to reduce false positive referrals.
